---
title: "MLSys Playground"
subtitle: "Four small games. Each one is a real ML systems concept made playable."
description: "Browser mini-games that turn ML systems concepts — pruning, the roofline model, GPU memory, mixed-precision quantization — into 30-second arcade loops."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
---

Four little games. Each one takes about a minute to play and quietly teaches one thing about how ML systems actually work. They share a palette and a vibe with the [ML Systems textbook](/) so they feel like part of the same world.

::: {.mlsp-game-cards}

::: {.mlsp-game-card}
### Pulse Prune
[Click the dim weights. Keep the bright ones.]{.mlsp-card-tagline}

A neural network on a canvas. You've got 45 seconds to prune 60% of the weights without crashing accuracy. Cut bright ones at your peril.

[▶ Play Pulse Prune](/games/prune.html){.mlsp-card-cta}
:::

::: {.mlsp-game-card}
### Roofline Runner
[Catch kernels under the ceiling.]{.mlsp-card-tagline}

Move a crosshair to catch falling kernels — but only the ones beneath the roofline. Catch one above and you're claiming throughput no hardware can deliver. 30-second rounds.

[▶ Play Roofline Runner](/games/roofline.html){.mlsp-card-cta}
:::

::: {.mlsp-game-card}
### OOM
[Pack tensors into HBM before you crash.]{.mlsp-card-tagline}

Tensor Tetris. Activations, gradients, optimizer states, and KV cache fall into your GPU's memory. Stack them right; complete rows free memory; overflow ends the run.

[▶ Play OOM](/games/oom.html){.mlsp-card-cta}
:::

::: {.mlsp-game-card}
### Quantization Cliff
[Dial layer precisions. Ship within budget.]{.mlsp-card-tagline}

Six layers, half your bit budget, three deploys. Find the precision allocation that keeps accuracy above 85%. Sensitivity is hidden — you learn it by failing.

[▶ Play Quantization Cliff](/games/quantization.html){.mlsp-card-cta}
:::

:::

---

*MLSys Playground is part of [mlsysbook.ai](/). The games share design vocabulary with the textbook — compute blue, data green, error red, MIT accent — so a figure in the book and a game in your browser feel like the same world.*
